schools.summary: Gill: when they began to work is not precisely said, but it is very probable they set about it directly, as soon as they were furnished with materials; the witnesses note Bezaleel, Aholiab, and every wise-hearted man, in whom the LORD had put wisdom, working all manner of work for the sanctuary according to all the LORD commanded — the beginning of the work by the Spirit-gifted men, all after the pattern (the gifted workmen now setting to the work: 'Then wrought Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding... according to all that the LORD had commanded'; the workmen, 'Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man,' the chief and his helpers and all the ingenious artificers under them; the gift, 'in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding to know how to work,' that their skill was God's putting, the wisdom to do the work being his gift; the diligence, that they set about it directly, as soon as they were furnished with materials, not delaying the work; the rule, 'according to all that the LORD had commanded,' that all was done exactly after the pattern, nothing of man's devising; that God gives both the gift and the command, and that the gifted are to use their gifts in his service and by his rule; that the work of God's house is wrought by Spirit-given skill, after God's own pattern).

themes: Then wrought Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding to know how to work all manner of work for the service of the sanctuary
according to all that the LORD had commanded
the Spirit-gifted men beginning the work of the sanctuary directly, all after the pattern
the work wrought by Spirit-given skill after God's pattern, God giving both the gift and the command

schools.summary: Gill: it was not enough that they had qualifications for this work, but they must have a call to it from Moses, to whom the whole affair was committed, to see it done according to the pattern; the witnesses note Moses calling Bezaleel, Aholiab, and every wise-hearted man whose heart the LORD had stirred up to come to the work — the calling of the gifted, joining God's gift, the willing heart, and Moses' call (gift, willingness, and call meeting in the workman: 'Moses called Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whose heart the LORD had put wisdom, even every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work'; the call, 'Moses called,' that it was not enough that they had qualifications but they must have a call to it from Moses, to whom the work was committed; the gift, 'in whose heart the LORD had put wisdom,' the God-given skill; the willingness, 'every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work,' that, besides the gift and the call, there was a willing heart, stirred up to the work; the three together, the gift of God, the inward willingness, and the outward call, all meeting in those who served; the lesson the commentators draw, that all that are concerned in God's work must have both the gift and the call, and a heart stirred to the work; that God fits, inclines, and calls those he employs).

debates.question: What three things met in those who came to the work of the sanctuary?

sides.position: God's gift (the LORD had put wisdom in their heart), the willing heart (whose heart stirred him up), and the outward call (Moses called) — all concerned in God's work must have both the gift and the call, and a heart stirred to the work, God fitting, inclining, and calling those he employs

themes: And Moses called Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whose heart the LORD had put wisdom
even every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work to do it
Moses calling the wise-hearted whose hearts the LORD had stirred up to the work
God's gift, the willing heart, and Moses' call meeting in the workman; God fitting, inclining, and calling those he employs

schools.summary: Gill: Bezaleel and Aholiab received it of Moses, who delivered it to the several workmen as their work required, to some gold, to others silver, brass, fine linen, and the rest; the witnesses note the workmen receiving from Moses all the offering the people had brought for the sanctuary, and the people still bringing free offerings every morning — the distributing of the materials and the daily flow of the people's willing gifts (the offerings coming in freely, morning by morning: 'they received of Moses all the offering... And they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning'; the receiving, 'received of Moses all the offering,' that the chief workmen took the materials from Moses, who delivered it to the several workmen as their work required, gold, silver, brass, linen, and the rest, each to his task; the continuing gifts, 'they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning,' that the people kept bringing their free offerings, willingly, every morning, a daily flow of liberality; the meaning the commentators draw, that the people's hearts were so stirred that they gave freely and continually, the work wanting nothing; that willing-hearted liberality keeps coming, morning by morning, a beautiful zeal for God's house; that God's work prospers where his people give freely).

themes: they received of Moses all the offering, which the children of Israel had brought for the work of the service of the sanctuary
And they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning
the workmen receiving the materials from Moses, the people still bringing free offerings every morning
the people's hearts so stirred that they gave freely and continually, the work wanting nothing

schools.summary: Gill: the ingenious artificers who were employed, some in one thing and some in another, either on the tabernacle itself or the vessels of it and things appertaining to it; the witnesses note all the wise men who wrought the work of the sanctuary coming, each from his own work, to Moses — the gathering of the workmen from their several tasks to speak with Moses (the workmen leaving their labour to bring word: 'all the wise men, that wrought all the work of the sanctuary, came every man from his work which they made'; the workmen, 'all the wise men, that wrought all the work,' the ingenious artificers employed, some in one thing, and some in another, each at his own part; the variety, that some wrought on the tabernacle itself, others on the vessels of it and things appertaining, every man at the task he was fittest for; the coming, 'came every man from his work,' that they left their labour and came together to Moses, to tell him of the abundance of the offerings; the meaning, that the work was carried by many hands, each in his place, all parts going forward together; that God's work is done by the joint labour of many, each fitted and busy in his own part; that the variety of gifts and tasks serves the one work).

themes: And all the wise men, that wrought all the work of the sanctuary
came every man from his work which they made
all the wise men coming, each from his own work, to Moses
God's work done by the joint labour of many, each fitted and busy in his own part, the variety of gifts serving the one work

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the workmen said unto Moses, The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work which the LORD commanded to make; the witnesses note the workmen, one in the name of the rest, telling Moses that the people brought much more than enough for the work — the rare report, that the people gave too much, a problem of overflowing willingness (the offerings exceeding the need: 'The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work, which the LORD commanded to make'; the speakers, that one in the name of the rest spoke for all the workmen; the report, 'the people bring much more than enough,' that the gifts brought in were more than the work required, the people's liberality overflowing; the rarity, that this is a strange and happy complaint, that there was too much given for God's work, a thing seldom seen; the meaning the commentators draw, that the people's hearts were so warmed and willing that their giving outran the need, a beautiful and rare zeal; that it is a blessed thing when the people's liberality must be restrained rather than urged; that a willing people will give beyond what is asked).

themes: And they spake unto Moses, saying, The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work
which the LORD commanded to make
the workmen reporting that the people brought much more than enough for the work
a rare and happy report; the people's willingness overflowing, a blessed thing when liberality must be restrained rather than urged

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Moses felt it necessary to interfere and forbid further offerings; by the expression 'Let neither man nor woman make any more work,' it would seem the superfluous offerings were of wrought work; the witnesses note Moses giving commandment, proclaimed through the camp, that none should bring any more for the sanctuary, so the people were restrained from bringing — the rare restraining of the people's liberality, their giving stopped because it was too much (Moses forbidding further gifts: 'Let neither man nor woman make any more work for the offering of the sanctuary. So the people were restrained from bringing'; the command, that Moses, finding it necessary to interfere, forbade further offerings, that none, man nor woman, should bring or make any more; the proclamation, 'caused it to be proclaimed throughout the camp,' that the order went out openly to all; the restraining, 'the people were restrained from bringing,' that, strange to say, the people had to be held back from giving, not urged to it; the meaning the commentators draw, that this was a singular honour to the people's zeal, their liberality so great it must be checked; that it is a rare and blessed thing when God's people must be restrained from giving rather than pressed to it; that the willing heart needs the bridle more than the spur).

debates.question: What is remarkable about the people being 'restrained from bringing'?

sides.position: It is a rare and blessed thing — that God's people had to be held back from giving rather than urged to it, their liberality so great it must be checked; a singular honour to their zeal, the willing heart needing the bridle more than the spur

themes: Let neither man nor woman make any more work for the offering of the sanctuary
So the people were restrained from bringing
Moses forbidding further offerings, so the people were restrained from bringing
a singular honour to the people's zeal; a rare and blessed thing when God's people need the bridle more than the spur in giving

schools.summary: Gill: the gold, silver, brass, precious stones, wood, and yarn already brought in were sufficient for everything they were ordered to make, and indeed too much; the witnesses note that the materials the people had brought were sufficient for all the work, and even too much — the abundant sufficiency of the offerings, the reason the people were restrained (there was enough and to spare for God's house: 'the stuff they had was sufficient for all the work to make it, and too much'; the sufficiency, 'sufficient for all the work to make it,' that the gold, silver, brass, precious stones, wood and yarn brought in were sufficient for everything they were ordered to make; the overplus, 'and too much,' that there was even more than was needed, an abundance beyond the work; the meaning the commentators draw, that the willing liberality of the people had provided not only enough but a surplus, so that the giving had to be stopped; that where the heart is willing, God's work shall not want, but be supplied with abundance; the rare honour, that the offerings were not too little but too much; that a free and willing people will more than supply the needs of God's house).

themes: For the stuff they had was sufficient for all the work to make it
and too much
the materials more than sufficient for all the work, even too much
the willing liberality of the people providing a surplus; where the heart is willing, God's work shall not want but be supplied with abundance

schools.summary: Gill: every wise-hearted man did the part assigned him, what he was fittest for and most skilful in; particularly some made ten curtains, which were properly the tabernacle; the witnesses note the wise-hearted men making the ten curtains of fine twined linen and the three colours, with cherubim of cunning work — the executing of the inner covering, the ten linen curtains, after the pattern (the curtains commanded now made: 'every wise hearted man... made ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet: with cherubims of cunning work made he them'; the workmen, 'every wise hearted man... did the part assigned him, what he was fittest for and most skilful in,' each at his task; the curtains, 'ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet,' the rich inner curtains, which were properly the tabernacle, made just as commanded; the figures, 'with cherubims of cunning work,' the embroidered cherubim, as appointed; that the making answered exactly to the command, the work done after the pattern; the type, as before, that the tabernacle of fine linen and colours with cherubim figures Christ, God's dwelling among men, and the church; that what God commanded was now faithfully wrought).

themes: every wise hearted man among them that wrought the work of the tabernacle made ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet
with cherubims of cunning work made he them
the wise-hearted men making the ten linen curtains with cherubim, just as commanded
the inner covering wrought after the pattern, the tabernacle figuring Christ, God's dwelling among men, and the church

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the length of one curtain was twenty and eight cubits, and the breadth four cubits, the curtains all of one size, then coupled five and five, with loops and golden taches, so it became one tabernacle; the witnesses note that each curtain was twenty-eight cubits long and four broad, all of one size, coupled by loops and golden taches into one tabernacle — the exact making of the curtains to the appointed measure, joined into one (the work answering the command in every measure: 'The length of one curtain was twenty and eight cubits... the curtains were all of one size'; the measure, 'twenty and eight cubits... and... four cubits,' the very dimensions commanded, each curtain alike; the uniformity, 'the curtains were all of one size,' all exactly alike, that they might join evenly; the joining, that, as the commentators relate, the five were coupled to five, with loops of blue and fifty taches of gold, so that it became one tabernacle; that the making followed the command exactly, measure for measure, nothing altered; the order and exactness of God's house, every part by precise rule after the pattern; the lesson, that the many curtains, joined by the golden taches, became one, a figure of the church made one).

themes: The length of one curtain was twenty and eight cubits, and the breadth of one curtain four cubits
the curtains were all of one size
each curtain made to the appointed measure, all of one size, joined by golden taches into one tabernacle
the work answering the command exactly; the many curtains becoming one, a figure of the church made one

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the shelter and special protection that the church is under are signified by the curtains of hair-cloth spread over the tabernacle, and the coverings of rams' skins and badgers' skins over them; God has provided for his people a shadow from the heat and a covert from storm and rain; the witnesses note the making of the eleven goats'-hair curtains for the tent over the tabernacle — the outer covering wrought after the pattern, the protection of God's house (the coverings signifying the church's shelter under God: 'he made curtains of goats' hair for the tent over the tabernacle: eleven curtains he made them'; the making, 'curtains of goats' hair... for the tent over the tabernacle,' the eleven curtains of goats' hair, made just as commanded, to cover the inner curtains; the great meaning Henry draws, that the shelter and special protection that the church is under are signified by the curtains of hair-cloth and the covering of rams' skins and badgers' skins, that God has provided for his people a shadow from the heat, and a covert from storm and rain; that God's people are armed against all weathers, protected from heat and tempest, and from the storms of divine wrath; that those who dwell in God's house find shelter, that, be the tempest ever so violent, it does not rain in; that the coverings of the tabernacle figure the safe shelter of the church under God's protection).

debates.question: What do the coverings spread over the tabernacle signify?

sides.position: The shelter and special protection that the church is under — God providing his people a shadow from the heat and a covert from storm and rain (Isaiah 4:6), armed against all weathers and from the storms of divine wrath; those who dwell in God's house finding that, be the tempest ever so violent, it does not rain in

themes: And he made curtains of goats' hair for the tent over the tabernacle
eleven curtains he made them
the eleven goats'-hair curtains made for the tent over the tabernacle, the protection of God's house
the coverings signifying the church's shelter under God, a shadow from the heat and a covert from storm, safe from all weathers

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: this verse corresponds exactly to Exodus 26:14, and relates the construction of the two outer coverings; the witnesses note the making of the two outermost coverings, of rams' skins dyed red and of badgers' skins above — the executing of the protective coverings after the pattern (the rams' and badgers' skins made as commanded: 'he made a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red, and a covering of badgers' skins above that'; that this corresponds exactly to Exodus 26:14, the command now carried out; the third covering, 'rams' skins dyed red,' the skins of rams dyed red, set over the goats'-hair; the fourth and outermost, 'badgers' skins above that,' the plain, weather-proof covering outermost; the type drawn before, that the rams' skins dyed red shadow the sufferings and atoning blood of Christ, and the plain badgers' skins his lowly outward appearance, who hath no form nor comeliness, the glory hidden within; that the making answered the command exactly, the work done after the pattern; that all the coverings, inner and outer, were faithfully wrought).

themes: And he made a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red
and a covering of badgers' skins above that
the two outermost coverings made just as commanded
the rams' skins dyed red shadowing Christ's atoning blood, the badgers' skins his lowly outward appearance, the glory hidden within

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the boards, referring to the directions given before for their making (Exodus 26:15), the walls of the tabernacle of shittim wood, set upright; the witnesses note the making of the boards of shittim wood, standing up, for the tabernacle — the executing of the upright frame after the pattern, the strength of God's house (the boards commanded now made: 'he made boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood, standing up'; the making, 'boards... of shittim wood, standing up,' the boards of incorruptible acacia wood, set upright, as commanded, forming the walls of the tabernacle; that this answered exactly to the former direction, the command now carried out; the great meaning the commentators draw, as Henry observed on the boards and bars, that the strength and stability of the church, though it is but a tabernacle, are signified by the boards; the type before given, that the boards of incorruptible wood, overlaid with gold and standing upright, shadow the saints, made firm and incorruptible by grace, upright and standing in God's house; that what God commanded was now faithfully wrought).

themes: And he made boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood
standing up
the boards of incorruptible shittim wood made and set upright, just as commanded
the strength and stability of the church signified by them; the boards shadowing the saints, made firm by grace, upright in God's house

schools.summary: Gill: the sockets of silver, the bases on which the boards stood, were properly the foundation of the tabernacle, two under each board for its two tenons; the witnesses note the making of the forty silver sockets, two under each board, for the boards of the second side — the executing of the silver foundation after the pattern, the boards standing fast (the sockets commanded now made: 'their forty sockets of silver; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board'; the sockets, 'forty sockets of silver,' the bases, properly the foundation of the tabernacle, two for each board, made as commanded; the fitting, 'two sockets under one board... and... another,' each board's two tenons set into two silver sockets, that it might stand firm; the type before given, that the silver sockets, made of the redemption-money, shadow Christ's redemption as the foundation on which the church stands and is established; that the boards rested wholly on silver, the whole house on the foundation of redemption; that the making answered the command exactly, the work done after the pattern; that what God commanded was faithfully wrought). Note: the source for this verse carried a cross-reference note on the feasts, not pertaining to the boards; the synthesis follows the verse's actual subject, the silver sockets.

themes: And their forty sockets of silver
two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board
the forty silver sockets made, two under each board, just as commanded
the silver foundation shadowing Christ's redemption, the whole house resting on the foundation of redemption

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'under every board two sockets' is undoubtedly the true meaning, the eight boards of the back of the tabernacle resting each on two silver sockets; the witnesses note the eight boards and their sixteen silver sockets, two under every board, for the west end — the executing of the west wall and its foundation after the pattern (the back wholly framed and founded as commanded: 'there were eight boards; and their sockets were sixteen sockets of silver, under every board two sockets'; the boards, 'eight boards,' the six boards and the two corner boards of the west end together, made as commanded; the sockets, 'sixteen sockets of silver, under every board two sockets,' two silver sockets under each of the eight boards, the back also founded on silver; that the west wall, like the rest, rested wholly on the silver foundation, the whole house on silver; the type before given, the silver foundation shadowing Christ's redemption, on which every part of the church is established; that the making answered the command, the work done after the pattern; that God's house is complete and founded on every side).

themes: And there were eight boards; and their sockets were sixteen sockets of silver
under every board two sockets
the eight boards of the west end and their sixteen silver sockets, just as commanded
the back also founded on silver, the silver shadowing Christ's redemption, God's house complete and founded on every side

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the bars, referring to the directions given before (Exodus 26:26), the long poles that held the boards together and firm; the witnesses note the making of the bars of shittim wood, five for the boards of the one side — the executing of the bars after the pattern, that bound the boards into a firm wall (the bars commanded now made: 'he made bars of shittim wood; five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle'; the bars, 'bars of shittim wood... five for the boards of the one side,' the long poles of incorruptible wood, five to that wall, made as commanded, to bind the boards; that, as before, the bars held the boards together and firm, joining the upright boards into one solid wall; the great meaning the commentators draw, as Henry observed, that the boards were coupled together and joined by the bars which shot through them, for the union of the church, and the hearty agreement of those that are its stays and supporters, contribute abundantly to its strength and establishment; that the bars figure the bond of love and the Spirit that binds the saints into one firm whole; that the making answered the command, the work done after the pattern).

debates.question: What do the bars that bound the boards together signify?

sides.position: The union of the church and the hearty agreement of those that are its stays and supporters, which contribute abundantly to its strength and establishment — the bond of love and the Spirit that binds the saints into one firm whole

themes: And he made bars of shittim wood
five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle
the bars of shittim wood made, five to a wall, binding the upright boards into one firm wall
the bars figuring the union of the church and the bond of love that binds the saints into one, contributing to its strength

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'for the sides westward,' rather for the back of the tabernacle westward, the bars for the back wall as well as the second side; the witnesses note the making of five bars for the other long side and five for the west end of the tabernacle — the executing of the bars for every wall after the pattern, that all might be firm (each wall bound by its bars as commanded: 'five bars for the boards of the other side... and five bars for the boards... for the sides westward'; the second side, 'five bars for the boards of the other side,' that the north wall too was bound by five bars; the west end, 'five bars for the boards... for the sides westward,' better for the back of the tabernacle, that the back wall too had its bars; that every wall, south, north, and west, was bound alike by five bars, all made firm by the same means, as commanded; that no wall was left unbound, the whole frame braced on every side; the meaning before given, that in God's house every part is alike bound and held firm, none left loose, the whole knit together; that the making answered the command exactly). Note: the source for this verse carried a cross-reference note on the kid-in-milk prohibition, not pertaining to the bars; the synthesis follows the verse's actual subject, the bars of the walls.

themes: And five bars for the boards of the other side of the tabernacle
and five bars for the boards of the tabernacle for the sides westward
five bars made for the north wall and the back westward, just as commanded
every wall bound alike, no wall left unbound, the whole frame braced on every side, the whole knit together

schools.summary: Matthew Henry and John Wesley: the veil made for a partition between the holy place and the most holy signified the darkness and distance of that dispensation, compared with the New Testament, which shows us the glory of God more clearly and invites us to draw near, and the darkness and distance of our present state compared with heaven, where we shall be ever with the Lord and see him as he is; the witnesses note the making of the veil of the rich colours and fine linen, with cherubim of cunning work — the executing of the veil after the pattern, that divided the holy from the most holy (the veil signifying the darkness and distance of the old dispensation: 'he made a veil of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen: with cherubims made he it of cunning work'; the making, the rich veil, as commanded, to part the holy place from the most holy; the great meaning Henry and Wesley draw, that the veil signified the darkness and distance of that dispensation, compared with the New Testament, which shows us the glory of God more clearly and invites us to draw near; the further sense, that it signified also the darkness and distance of our present state, in comparison with heaven, where we shall be ever with the Lord and see him as he is; the type before given, that the veil shadows the flesh of Christ, rent at his death, opening the way to God; that the making answered the command exactly).

debates.question: What did the veil between the holy place and the most holy signify?

sides.position: The darkness and distance of that dispensation, compared with the New Testament, which shows the glory of God more clearly and invites us to draw near; and the distance of our present state compared with heaven, where we shall see him as he is; the veil also shadowing the flesh of Christ (Hebrews 10:20), rent at his death

themes: And he made a veil of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen
with cherubims made he it of cunning work
the rich veil made with cherubim, dividing the holy from the most holy
signifying the darkness and distance of that dispensation, the clearer New Testament inviting us to draw near; the veil shadowing the flesh of Christ

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: there was a veil made for the door of the tabernacle, at which the people assembled, though forbidden to enter; for, while we are in this present state, we must get as near to God as we can; the witnesses note the making of the hanging for the door of the tabernacle, of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, of needlework — the executing of the entrance-screen after the pattern, the door of God's house (the hanging made for the door where the people assembled: 'he made an hanging for the tabernacle door of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, of needlework'; the making, the rich screen at the entrance, as commanded, of the same colours as the veil though of plainer needlework, without the cherubim; the meaning Henry draws, that at this door the people assembled, though forbidden to enter, for, while we are in this present state, we must get as near to God as we can; the type before given, that the door-hanging shadows Christ the door, by whom we enter into God's house and worship; that there is one appointed way in, and that the people are to draw as near to God as they may; that the making answered the command).

themes: And he made an hanging for the tabernacle door of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen
of needlework
the rich hanging made for the tabernacle door, the entrance where the people assembled
while we are in this present state, we must get as near to God as we can; the door-hanging shadowing Christ the door

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the five pillars with their hooks, their capitals overlaid with gold, made as before directed (Exodus 26:32, 37); there is scarcely anything in this chapter not touched on before, it being a record of the execution of what was commanded, to show that all was done according to the pattern; the witnesses note the making of the five pillars of the door, with their hooks, their tops and fillets overlaid with gold, on five sockets of brass — the executing of the supports of the door after the pattern, and the faithful record that all was done as commanded (the door resting on golden-topped pillars set on brass: 'the five pillars of it with their hooks: and he overlaid their chapiters and their fillets with gold: but their five sockets were of brass'; the pillars, 'five pillars... with their hooks,' the five pillars of the entrance, with their golden hooks, made as commanded; the gold, 'overlaid their chapiters and their fillets with gold,' the tops and bands gilded; the sockets, 'their five sockets were of brass,' that these stood on brass, not silver like the veil's pillars, the brass befitting the outer entrance; the great point Clarke draws, that the chapter, like those following, is a repetition of what we have already met in the commands, recording the execution of it to show that all was done according to the pattern shown to Moses; that the faithful obedience of the workmen is recorded, that nothing was wanting; that God would have it known that all was done exactly as he commanded).

debates.question: Why is the execution of the work recorded in such detail, repeating the commands?

sides.position: To show that all was done according to the pattern shown to Moses — recording the workmen's faithful obedience, that nothing was wanting and all was done exactly as God commanded

themes: And the five pillars of it with their hooks: and he overlaid their chapiters and their fillets with gold
but their five sockets were of brass
the five pillars of the door with golden tops set on five sockets of brass, befitting the outer entrance
the chapter recording the execution of what was commanded, that all was done according to the pattern shown to Moses
